verses.text: And it came to pass, after the year was expired, at the time when kings go forth to battle, that David sent Joab, and his servants with him, and all Israel; and they destroyed the children of Ammon, and besieged Rabbah. But David tarried still at Jerusalem. Guidance: The first verse is the chapter's opening accusation. 'After the year was expired' — i.e., the next spring, after the rainy season ends and military campaigns become possible. 'At the time when kings go forth to battle' — the narrator names the cultural expectation: kings personally led their armies in the spring campaign season. David sends Joab, his servants, 'and all Israel.' The army marches; the Ammonite war from chapter 10 resumes; Joab besieges Rabbah, the Ammonite capital. The chapter's last clause is the verdict: 'But David tarried still at Jerusalem.' David has stayed home. The Hebrew construction makes it emphatic — David is the odd man out; everyone else is at the front. The chapter is going to show us what happens to a king who is at home when he should be at war. The trouble begins with idleness. The narrator does not say David is wrong to stay home; he simply records that David is at home when kings are out. The very next verse will show what idleness produces. The chapter is teaching us that vocation has a season. When the season comes for the work, the work is the safest place to be. Skipping the work does not produce extra rest; it produces extra exposure to temptations the work would have prevented.

And it came to pass in an eveningtide, that David arose from off his bed, and walked upon the roof of the king's house: and from the roof he saw a woman washing herself; and the woman was very beautiful to look upon. Guidance: Evening. David rises from his bed — the Hebrew implies an afternoon nap; he has slept through the heat of the day. He goes up to the palace roof — flat-roofed Israelite palaces were the high-points of the city, used for cooler evening air and panoramic views. From there he sees a woman washing. The Hebrew rochetset suggests a ceremonial purification bath after menstruation (the chapter will note this in v4), which would have been done in a domestic courtyard not visible from the street but visible from above. Bathsheba is not exposing herself publicly; the king's roof has a sightline her own household assumed nobody would be using. 'The woman was very beautiful to look upon.' The narrator pauses to register what David sees. The chapter is careful: the seeing is not the sin. Anyone walking the roof at that hour would have seen what David saw. The sin begins in what David does with what he sees. He does not turn away; he does not return downstairs. He stops and looks. He takes the next step. The chapter is teaching the basic moral grammar of the eye-and-heart sequence: seeing happens; lingering is choice; investigating is the next choice; pursuing is the choice after that. David is about to take every choice.

And David sent and enquired after the woman. And one said, Is not this Bath-sheba, the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite? Guidance: David sends. The same word the chapter has used and will use many times — David sent, David sent, David sent. He uses the messengers and prerogatives of royal office to extract personal information. The reply is one of the most devastating verses in 2 Samuel because it contains, in the form of an answer, three checks David should have heeded. (1) 'Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam.' Eliam appears in 23:34 as one of David's mighty men — David's elite warriors. Bathsheba is the daughter of one of his most loyal officers. (2) Some scholars (and the Talmud) identify this Eliam as the son of Ahithophel — making Bathsheba Ahithophel's granddaughter. If correct, this explains Ahithophel's later defection to Absalom (15:31; 16:23) — the family grudge against David traces to this moment. (3) 'The wife of Uriah the Hittite.' Uriah is also in the mighty men list (23:39). David is being told, in one sentence, that he is about to sin against a granddaughter, a daughter, and a wife of three men loyal to him. The information is supposed to stop him. It does not. The reply was, in effect, 'this woman has the strongest possible network of reasons not to be touched.' David hears it and proceeds anyway. Lust at its furthest gone is the willingness to ignore every relational tie that ought to function as a brake.

And David sent messengers, and took her; and she came in unto him, and he lay with her; for she was purified from her uncleanness: and she returned unto her house. And the woman conceived, and sent and told David, and said, I am with child. Guidance: The narrator describes the act in the briefest possible Hebrew — six verbs, no adverbs, no description. The economy is moral; the chapter is not lingering. 'David sent messengers, and took her' — the king's officers retrieve a married woman from her home and bring her to the palace. The Hebrew verb laqach ('took') is the same word used of taking a woman to be a wife — and David is not making her his wife; he is taking her without that legal step. The verse does not engage the modern question of whether Bathsheba had power to refuse a royal summons (probably she did not). What the verse does engage is David's responsibility. The chapter, from here to the end, will lay the moral weight on him. 'He lay with her; for she was purified from her uncleanness' — the parenthetical detail is critical. The note about her ritual purification confirms that the child she will conceive is David's, not Uriah's; she was past menstruation, in the fertile window, and Uriah had been away at war. The biological fact will be undeniable. 'And she returned unto her house.' She goes home. Days or weeks pass. Then: 'The woman conceived, and sent and told David, and said, I am with child.' Bathsheba's four-word message — five words in Hebrew, hara anokhi — drops the entire weight of the chapter into David's lap. She has not asked him to do anything; she has only informed him. The next move is his. The chapter is going to show us what he does, and the cascade that follows.

And David sent messengers, and took her; and she came in unto him, and he lay with her; for she was purified from her uncleanness: and she returned unto her house. And the woman conceived, and sent and told David, and said, I am with child. Guidance: The narrator describes the act in the briefest possible Hebrew — six verbs, no adverbs, no description. The economy is moral; the chapter is not lingering. 'David sent messengers, and took her' — the king's officers retrieve a married woman from her home and bring her to the palace. And David sent to Joab, saying, Send me Uriah the Hittite. And Joab sent. And when Uriah was come unto him, David demanded of him how Joab did, and how the people did, and how the war prospered. Guidance: David's cover-up plan. He sends to Joab to send him Uriah. Joab does not ask why; the king does not explain. Uriah arrives — pulled from the siege at Rabbah, four days' journey south to Jerusalem. He stands before David. David asks the small-talk questions: 'how Joab did, how the people did, how the war prospered.' Three questions, all military, all surface. The chapter is showing us a king dissembling. He has no real interest in the answers — he has just summoned a senior officer from the front lines for what he is about to disguise as an unimportant social visit. The hypocrisy is layered. The man whose home is not the king's responsibility, whose wife is the king's secret, is being received with friendly questions about a war the king himself should be at.

And David sent to Joab, saying, Send me Uriah the Hittite. And Joab sent. And when Uriah was come unto him, David demanded of him how Joab did, and how the people did, and how the war prospered. Guidance: David's cover-up plan. He sends to Joab to send him Uriah. Joab does not ask why; the king does not explain. Uriah arrives — pulled from the siege at Rabbah, four days' journey south to Jerusalem. He stands before David. David asks the small-talk questions: 'how Joab did, how the people did, how the war prospered.' Three questions, all military, all surface. The chapter is showing us a king dissembling. He has no real interest in the answers — he has just summoned a senior officer from the front lines for what he is about to disguise as an unimportant social visit. The hypocrisy is layered. The man whose home is not the king's responsibility, whose wife is the king's secret, is being received with friendly questions about a war the king himself should be at.

And David said to Uriah, Go down to thy house, and wash thy feet. And Uriah departed out of the king's house, and there followed him a mess of meat from the king. Guidance: David's instruction: 'Go down to thy house, and wash thy feet.' 'Wash thy feet' is Israelite shorthand for the end of a journey — relax, settle in. The implication: go sleep with your wife tonight. The plan is for Uriah to consummate the marital reunion so that the pregnancy already in progress can be attributed to him. The royal gift — 'a mess of meat from the king' (a portion from the royal table) — accompanies him to encourage hospitality and the night's intended atmosphere. The chapter is showing us the elaborate machinery David is willing to construct to cover the original sin. Concealment is sin's natural sequel; David, having taken Bathsheba, is now arranging the appearance of legitimacy for the child he fathered. The deeper sin of the chapter is not the adultery (already named); it is the cover-up. The cover-up will require killing Uriah.

But Uriah slept at the door of the king's house with all the servants of his lord, and went not down to his house. And when they had told David, saying, Uriah went not down unto his house, David said unto Uriah, Camest thou not from thy journey? why then didst thou not go down unto thine house? And Uriah said unto David, The ark, and Israel, and Judah, abide in tents; and my lord Joab, and the servants of my lord, are encamped in the open fields; shall I then go into mine house, to eat and to drink, and to lie with my wife? as thou livest, and as thy soul liveth, I will not do this thing. Guidance: Uriah does not go home. He sleeps at the palace gate with the king's servants — the position of a soldier on duty. The choice is deliberate. Either he has heard rumors of the king's interest in his wife (most probable — court gossip travels), or he has principle enough to refuse the comforts of home while his unit is at war (also true). The next morning, David asks why. Uriah's answer is one of the most morally devastating speeches in 2 Samuel. 'The ark, and Israel, and Judah, abide in tents.' The ark is at the front, in a portable tent; the army is in field-tents. 'My lord Joab, and the servants of my lord, are encamped in the open fields.' My comrades are sleeping on the ground. 'Shall I then go into mine house, to eat and to drink, and to lie with my wife?' Three pleasures of home — food, drink, marital intimacy — all refused for the sake of solidarity with the soldiers Uriah will return to. 'As thou livest, and as thy soul liveth, I will not do this thing.' The oath formula is the strongest one in Hebrew, and Uriah is swearing it on the king's own life. The irony is overwhelming. The Hittite mercenary — a foreigner, a convert, a soldier — embodies exactly the discipline and covenant solidarity that the king he is swearing by has abandoned. Uriah refuses to eat, drink, or sleep with his wife while the war is going on; David, while the war goes on, has slept with Uriah's wife. The chapter is using the foreigner Uriah to expose, by contrast, what David should have been. The Hittite has the Israelite virtue; the Israelite king does not. Uriah's faithfulness is not the chapter's only point — but it is, in this verse, an unanswerable rebuke.

And David said to Uriah, Tarry here today also, and tomorrow I will let thee depart. So Uriah abode in Jerusalem that day, and the morrow. And when David had called him, he did eat and drink before him; and he made him drunk: and at even he went out to lie on his bed with the servants of his lord, but went not down to his house. Guidance: Plan B. David delays Uriah another day and tries to break his resolve with alcohol. He invites Uriah to dinner and 'made him drunk.' The deliberate intoxication of a guest in order to compromise his judgment is one of the chapter's nastiest details. Yet even drunk, Uriah remains faithful. He sleeps with the servants again, not at his own house. Drunkenness has not eroded what sober resolve had set. The contrast deepens. Uriah, the foreign mercenary, drunk on the king's wine, is more faithful to his Israelite military discipline than the Israelite king is to his Israelite marriage. The chapter is rubbing it in by repetition. Two nights, two attempts, two refusals. David has now exhausted the diplomatic options. He moves to the murderous option.

And David said to Uriah, Tarry here today also, and tomorrow I will let thee depart. So Uriah abode in Jerusalem that day, and the morrow. And it came to pass in the morning, that David wrote a letter to Joab, and sent it by the hand of Uriah. And he wrote in the letter, saying, Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire ye from him, that he may be smitten, and die. Guidance: One of the darkest sentences in scripture. 'David wrote a letter to Joab, and sent it by the hand of Uriah.' The king writes the murder order on a sealed letter and entrusts the delivery to the victim. Uriah carries his own death warrant in his hand back to the front. The literacy and discretion of the messenger are weaponized against him; Uriah cannot read what he is carrying because the seal is the king's. The contents: 'Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire ye from him, that he may be smitten, and die.' The instruction is precise and unambiguous. Joab is to maneuver Uriah into the most dangerous part of the assault, then withdraw support so that Uriah cannot survive. The plan is essentially execution by friendly-fire — battlefield combat is the disguise, but the death is engineered. The chapter is showing us how far David has fallen. Adultery in private; deception of an honorable subordinate; deliberate intoxication of a guest; and now contract-murder by sealed letter carried by the target. Each step has made the next step necessary. The cover-up of one sin requires the commission of another. There is a category of moral decline in which each act is undertaken to disguise the previous act; the chain ends only when the original sinner finally repents, or when something fatal stops him. David's chain will not end until Nathan confronts him in chapter 12.

And it came to pass, when Joab observed the city, that he assigned Uriah unto a place where he knew that valiant men were. And the men of the city went out, and fought with Joab: and there fell some of the people of the servants of David; and Uriah the Hittite died also. Guidance: Joab carries out the order, but with his own modification. David had asked for Uriah to be put in front and then abandoned; Joab places him next to a position 'where he knew that valiant men were' — a sector where Ammonite defenders would mount a serious sortie. Joab does not pull troops back; he simply puts Uriah in the spot where Ammonite counter-attacks will produce maximum Israelite casualties. As predicted, the city's defenders sally out; some Israelites die; Uriah dies with them. The casualty list is plural — 'some of the people of the servants of David… and Uriah the Hittite died also.' Joab has covered the murder by ensuring it does not look like a targeted killing. Several Israelites die alongside Uriah, making the report look like ordinary siege losses. The detail is chilling. Joab's modification means David's sin has now produced additional unrelated deaths — Israelite soldiers killed in the maneuver designed to cover up Uriah's killing. The widows and orphans these men leave behind are also victims of David's chapter. Sin's collateral damage is always larger than the principal sinner intends. There is a category of sin whose cost is not paid by the sinner alone.

Then Joab sent and told David all the things concerning the war; And charged the messenger, saying, When thou hast made an end of telling the matters of the war unto the king, And if so be that the king's wrath arise, and he say unto thee, Wherefore approached ye so nigh unto the city when ye did fight? knew ye not that they would shoot from the wall? Who smote Abimelech the son of Jerubbesheth? did not a woman cast a piece of a millstone upon him from the wall, that he died in Thebez? why went ye nigh the wall? then say thou, Thy servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also. Guidance: Joab sends a messenger to David and prepares the messenger for the king's anticipated complaint. The whole apparatus of communication is now organized around managing David's reaction to a casualty report that David himself ordered. The detail Joab anticipates: David will ask 'why did your troops come so close to the wall? Don't you remember Abimelech in Judges 9 — killed at Thebez by a millstone dropped from a tower on top of him?' (The Judges 9 reference is famous — getting too close to a city wall is the soldier's elementary mistake.) Joab knows David could plausibly use this complaint as a cover for not being seen as the one who caused the deaths. So Joab gives the messenger the punchline: 'when the king's anger arises about the casualties, mention Uriah's death — that will quiet him.' Joab has read the king. He knows the casualties were the cover; he knows Uriah's name will absorb the question. The chapter is exposing the corruption Joab now sees in the king he serves. Joab understands the murder he is participating in, and he is calibrating his reporting to keep the king from looking guilty. The royal court has become a place where the senior general manages the king's public image around the assassination the king ordered. The institutional rot has set in. The chapter is showing us not just David's fall but the courtly corruption his fall is producing in those around him.

(The Judges 9 reference is famous — getting too close to a city wall is the soldier's elementary mistake.) Joab knows David could plausibly use this complaint as a cover for not being seen as the one who caused the deaths. So Joab gives the messenger the punchline: 'when the king's anger arises about the casualties, mention Uriah's death — that will quiet him.' Joab has read the king. He knows the casualties were the cover; he knows Uriah's name will absorb the question. The chapter is exposing the corruption Joab now sees in the king he serves. Joab understands the murder he is participating in, and he is calibrating his reporting to keep the king from looking guilty. The royal court has become a place where the senior general manages the king's public image around the assassination the king ordered. The institutional rot has set in. The chapter is showing us not just David's fall but the courtly corruption his fall is producing in those around him.

So the messenger went, and came and shewed David all that Joab had sent him for. And the messenger said unto David, Surely the men prevailed against us, and came out unto us into the field, and we were upon them even unto the entering of the gate. And the shooters shot from off the wall upon thy servants; and some of the king's servants be dead, and thy servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also. Guidance: The messenger arrives in Jerusalem and delivers the report. The text shortens the conversation — David apparently does not raise the Abimelech-style objection that Joab anticipated, so the messenger goes straight to the heart of Joab's prepared brief. He names the casualties and adds the punchline Joab gave him: 'thy servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also.' The same name that justifies the casualty count in David's mind is the name that gives David what he wanted. The messenger does not know the inner meaning of his closing line; he just knows it was important. The chapter is showing us how thoroughly compartmentalized the moral fact of the chapter has become. The messenger does not know he is delivering the news David has been waiting for; Joab knows but does not say; David hears and (the next verse will show) does not break stride. The murder has been committed and reported, with each link in the chain doing its small part while the principal sinner sits in the palace and listens.

Then David said unto the messenger, Thus shalt thou say unto Joab, Let not this thing displease thee, for the sword devoureth one as well as another: make thy battle more strong against the city, and overthrow it: and encourage thou him. Guidance: David's reply is one of the most cold-blooded sentences in 2 Samuel. 'Let not this thing displease thee, for the sword devoureth one as well as another.' The casual fatalism — the sword eats whomever the sword eats — is the philosophy of a man trying very hard not to be the principal cause of one specific death. He frames the entire Uriah killing as a regrettable accident in the general randomness of war. 'Make thy battle more strong against the city, and overthrow it.' Carry on with the siege. 'And encourage thou him' — buck Joab up; tell him not to take the casualties personally. The chapter is showing us a king who has just been notified that his murder plan worked, and whose response is to brush it off as ordinary attrition. He has not even allowed himself a private moment of recognition. The deep numbness of an unconfessed sin: the sinner does not feel the weight of what he has done, because feeling it would require him to face what he has done. The face-saving narrative — 'the sword devours one as well as another' — is the protective lie David is now telling himself. The chapter is one of the most realistic pictures in scripture of a heart hardening in real time. Nathan in chapter 12 is going to break the lie with one parable; until then, David is going to live inside it.

And when the wife of Uriah heard that Uriah her husband was dead, she mourned for her husband. And when the mourning was past, David sent and fetched her to his house, and she became his wife, and bare him a son. But the thing that David had done displeased the LORD. Guidance: Bathsheba mourns. The standard Israelite mourning period is seven days (sometimes thirty for a major figure). The narrator does not romanticize her grief; he records it without comment. We do not know how much Bathsheba knew of the chain of events from the night David sent for her to the report of her husband's death. We know that her mourning is real (the verb saphad is genuine lament), and then the mourning ends and David acts. 'David sent and fetched her to his house, and she became his wife, and bare him a son.' The legal cover is now in place: she is a widow; David has married a widow; the child she bore — born about six months after the wedding — could plausibly be presented as a slightly-premature child of the new marriage. The political problem is solved. Bathsheba is now Bathsheba the wife of David. The chapter could have ended here with the cover-up complete. It does not. The final clause of the chapter is one of the great single sentences of the Old Testament: 'But the thing that David had done displeased the LORD.' Hebrew vayyeira hadabar asher-asah David be'eyne YHWH — 'and the thing that David had done was evil in the eyes of the LORD.' The legal cover that fools the political world does not fool God. The whole apparatus of concealment — the cover marriage, the death-by-letter, the drunken deception, the secret coupling — is naked before the LORD. He has watched every step. The chapter ends with a single eight-word indictment that prepares for chapter 12. Nathan is about to be sent. The same God who saw David's faithful dance with the ark in chapter 6 has now seen everything else in chapter 11. The LORD's verdict on David's life is not the verdict of the palace gossip; it is the verdict of heaven. The chapter ends with the curtain pulled back — God sees what no one else has seen — and the next chapter will begin the consequences. The 'displeased the LORD' clause is the most important sentence in the chapter; everything that follows in 2 Samuel — Bathsheba's child's death, Tamar's rape, Absalom's rebellion, Solomon's eventual succession — flows from this verdict.
